Which of the following hemodynamic changes best explains the clinical deterioration in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reversal of shunt flow to right-to-left due to irreversible pulmonary vascular disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development of infundibular right ventricular outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Spontaneous closure of the ventricular septal defect with right heart failure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Superimposed rheumatic mitral stenosis increasing left atrial pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Progressive aortic root dilation causing aortic regurgitation

explanation.summary: This patient has developed Eisenmenger syndrome—irreversible pulmonary hypertension from chronic left-to-right shunting that has caused pulmonary vascular remodeling and reversal of shunt direction to right-to-left, producing cyanosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Years of excessive pulmonary blood flow from a large VSD causes progressive pulmonary vascular remodeling (medial hypertrophy, intimal fibrosis, plexiform lesions). When pulmonary vascular resistance exceeds systemic resistance, the shunt reverses to right-to-left (Eisenmenger physiology). The murmur diminishes because equalization of pressures reduces the transventricular gradient. Surgical repair is contraindicated at this stage.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Infundibular RVOT obstruction occurs in tetralogy of Fallot, not in an isolated VSD. The clinical picture of loud P2 and cyanosis with a diminished VSD murmur is characteristic of Eisenmenger syndrome, not acquired subpulmonic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Spontaneous VSD closure occurs in small muscular defects during childhood, not in large defects in adulthood. The diminished murmur here reflects pressure equalization, not defect closure. Also, this would not explain cyanosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rheumatic mitral stenosis would cause left atrial hypertension and pulmonary congestion, presenting with orthopnea and hemoptysis. It would not explain the right-to-left shunting, clubbing, and diminished VSD murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic regurgitation from aortic root dilation can occur with subarterial VSDs but would present with a diastolic decrescendo murmur, bounding pulses, and wide pulse pressure—not cyanosis, clubbing, and right ventricular dominance.

questions.stem: A premature infant born at 28 weeks gestation is now 10 days old and on mechanical ventilation for respiratory distress syndrome. Despite surfactant therapy, the infant has worsening respiratory status with increasing FiO2 requirements. Examination reveals bounding peripheral pulses, a hyperactive precordium, and a continuous murmur at the left infraclavicular area. The ventilator shows decreased pulmonary compliance. Which of the following best explains the worsening respiratory status in this premature infant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulmonary overcirculation from a hemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progressive surfactant deficiency due to type II pneumocyte immaturity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Development of necrotizing enterocolitis with sepsis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia from prolonged oxygen exposure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tension pneumothorax from barotrauma

explanation.summary: The continuous murmur, bounding pulses, hyperactive precordium, and widened pulse pressure in a premature infant are classic signs of a hemodynamically significant PDA causing pulmonary overcirculation and respiratory deterioration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Premature infants are particularly susceptible to PDA because the ductus arteriosus is maintained by prostaglandins and is less responsive to oxygen-mediated closure due to immaturity. A significant left-to-right shunt through the PDA floods the pulmonary vasculature, decreases lung compliance, increases ventilator requirements, and causes a 'steal' phenomenon from systemic circulation (bounding pulses from diastolic runoff).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Surfactant deficiency typically improves after exogenous surfactant administration, not worsens at day 10. Progressive deterioration with new cardiac findings points to a hemodynamic cause rather than recurrent surfactant deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Necrotizing enterocolitis presents with abdominal distension, bilious emesis, and bloody stools. While it can cause sepsis and respiratory deterioration, it would not explain the continuous murmur and bounding pulses.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia is a chronic process that develops over weeks of mechanical ventilation and oxygen therapy. At 10 days, it is too early for established BPD, and it would not explain the new cardiac findings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tension pneumothorax causes acute deterioration with unilateral absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, and hemodynamic compromise. It would not cause a continuous murmur or bounding pulses.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant with a moderate perimembranous ventricular septal defect is being followed by cardiology. The parents report that the infant has been breathing rapidly and sweating during feeds for the past 3 weeks. Current weight is below the 5th percentile. Chest radiograph shows cardiomegaly and pulmonary edema. After stabilization with diuretics and ACE inhibitors, the infant continues to have failure to thrive. Echocardiography shows a 7-mm perimembranous VSD with a Qp:Qs of 2.5:1 and estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 50 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue medical therapy and reassess in 6 months for spontaneous closure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform surgical VSD closure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place a pulmonary artery band as a palliative procedure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiate bosentan for pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform transcatheter device closure of the VSD

explanation.summary: This infant has a large, hemodynamically significant VSD with refractory heart failure despite medical therapy, failure to thrive, and elevated pulmonary artery pressures. Definitive surgical closure is indicated to prevent irreversible pulmonary vascular disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Surgical VSD repair is indicated when there is a hemodynamically significant shunt (Qp:Qs ≥ 2:1) with heart failure refractory to medical management, failure to thrive, or elevated pulmonary artery pressures. Early repair (typically by 6 months of age) prevents the development of irreversible pulmonary vascular disease (Eisenmenger syndrome). Surgical closure has excellent outcomes with low mortality (<2%) in experienced centers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Watchful waiting is appropriate for small, hemodynamically insignificant VSDs. This infant has a large defect with refractory heart failure and pulmonary hypertension; continued observation risks irreversible pulmonary vascular changes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary artery banding was historically used to limit pulmonary blood flow, but it is now reserved for complex anatomy or multiple muscular VSDs where primary closure is technically difficult. A single perimembranous VSD should be closed primarily.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bosentan (endothelin receptor antagonist) is used for established pulmonary arterial hypertension but is not appropriate here. The pulmonary hypertension is secondary to excessive left-to-right shunting and will resolve with defect closure, not with pulmonary vasodilator therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transcatheter device closure of perimembranous VSDs is technically challenging due to proximity to the aortic valve and conduction system. It is not standard of care for perimembranous VSDs in infants; surgical closure remains the preferred approach.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents for a routine preconception visit. She reports no symptoms and exercises regularly. On examination, a grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur is heard at the left upper sternal border with a fixed, widely split S2. ECG shows an incomplete right bundle branch block and right axis deviation. She asks about the long-term risks of her condition if left untreated. Which of the following is the most common long-term complication of an unrepaired secundum atrial septal defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Infective endocarditis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete heart block

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ventricular septal rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic root dilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation and flutter

explanation.summary: Unrepaired ASD causes chronic right atrial and right ventricular volume overload, which over decades leads to atrial dilation and an increasing incidence of atrial arrhythmias, particularly atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter. These are the most common long-term complications.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic right atrial volume overload from the left-to-right shunt causes progressive atrial dilation and structural remodeling. By age 40, over 50% of patients with unrepaired ASDs develop atrial fibrillation or flutter. Atrial arrhythmias can precipitate right heart failure and increase the risk of paradoxical embolism and stroke through the ASD.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Isolated secundum ASDs carry an extremely low risk of infective endocarditis, unlike VSDs or PDAs. Endocarditis prophylaxis is not recommended for unrepaired ASDs. The low-velocity shunt does not create the turbulent jet that predisposes to endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Complete heart block is not a complication of secundum ASD. However, primum ASD (part of an atrioventricular septal defect) can be associated with first-degree AV block due to the inferior displacement of the AV node.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ventricular septal rupture is a mechanical complication of acute myocardial infarction, not a complication of ASD. The ventricular septum is intact in an isolated ASD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic root dilation is associated with bicuspid aortic valve, Marfan syndrome, and other connective tissue disorders. ASD involves right-sided volume overload and does not affect the aortic root.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old infant born at 26 weeks gestation is on mechanical ventilation in the NICU. Echocardiography confirms a patent ductus arteriosus with significant left-to-right shunting. Two courses of intravenous ibuprofen have failed to close the ductus. The infant continues to have worsening respiratory failure and hemodynamic instability. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Third course of intravenous ibuprofen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical ligation of the ductus arteriosus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral acetaminophen therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhaled nitric oxide therapy

explanation.summary: After failure of two courses of pharmacologic therapy (indomethacin or ibuprofen), surgical ligation is the definitive treatment for a hemodynamically significant PDA causing clinical deterioration in a premature infant.
explanation.whyCorrect: Surgical PDA ligation is indicated when pharmacologic closure fails (typically after 2 courses of COX inhibitors) and the infant remains hemodynamically compromised. The procedure involves left thoracotomy with clip ligation or division of the ductus and has a high success rate with low morbidity in experienced centers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A third course of COX inhibitor therapy has diminishing returns and increased risk of adverse effects (renal impairment, necrotizing enterocolitis, platelet dysfunction) without significantly improving closure rates after two failed courses.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Prostaglandin E1 maintains ductal patency and would directly worsen the clinical situation by keeping the ductus open and increasing the left-to-right shunt.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oral acetaminophen has emerging evidence as an alternative agent for PDA closure, but in an infant with hemodynamic instability after two failed pharmacologic courses, definitive surgical intervention is warranted rather than trialing another medical therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inhaled nitric oxide is a pulmonary vasodilator used for persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn. It would decrease pulmonary vascular resistance and could increase the left-to-right shunt through the PDA, worsening the condition.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl is found to have an asymptomatic heart murmur during a routine sports physical. Examination reveals a grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border, a widely split and fixed S2, and a soft mid-diastolic murmur at the left lower sternal border. Echocardiography shows a 20-mm secundum ASD with a Qp:Qs of 2.2:1 and normal pulmonary artery pressures. All four rims of the defect are adequate. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Observation with serial echocardiograms every 2 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Endocarditis prophylaxis and activity restriction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Open surgical repair with cardiopulmonary bypass

choices.label: D
choices.text: Percutaneous transcatheter device closure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Medical therapy with diuretics and ACE inhibitors

explanation.summary: This patient has a hemodynamically significant secundum ASD (Qp:Qs > 1.5:1) with adequate rims for device deployment. Percutaneous transcatheter closure is the preferred approach for secundum ASDs with appropriate anatomy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transcatheter closure using devices such as the Amplatzer septal occluder is the standard of care for secundum ASDs with adequate septal rims (≥5 mm from surrounding structures). It avoids sternotomy, has shorter recovery time, fewer complications, and equivalent closure rates compared to surgery. Closure is indicated when Qp:Qs ≥ 1.5:1 to prevent long-term atrial arrhythmias and right heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Observation alone is not appropriate for a hemodynamically significant ASD (Qp:Qs > 2:1). Without closure, chronic right heart volume overload will lead to atrial arrhythmias, right heart failure, and potentially Eisenmenger syndrome in adulthood.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Endocarditis prophylaxis is not recommended for isolated ASDs. Activity restriction is unnecessary in the absence of pulmonary hypertension or arrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surgical repair is effective but is reserved for ASDs not amenable to device closure (e.g., primum ASDs, sinus venosus defects, inadequate rims, or very large defects). This secundum ASD with adequate rims is ideal for transcatheter closure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Medical therapy does not close the defect and only provides temporary symptomatic relief. It does not prevent the long-term complications of chronic right heart volume overload.

questions.stem: A 1-week-old infant is brought to the emergency department with poor feeding, lethargy, and mottled skin. On examination, femoral pulses are absent bilaterally. Upper extremity blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg (right arm) and lower extremity blood pressure is 50/30 mmHg (right leg). A grade 2/6 systolic murmur is heard over the left interscapular area. Oxygen saturation is 98% in the right hand and 85% in the left foot. The infant becomes acutely worse when prostaglandin E1 is discontinued in the referring hospital. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interrupted aortic arch

choices.label: B
choices.text: Large ventricular septal defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Critical coarctation of the aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return

explanation.summary: This neonate presents with classic findings of critical (ductal-dependent) coarctation of the aorta: absent femoral pulses, upper-to-lower extremity blood pressure gradient, differential cyanosis (higher saturation in right arm than feet), and acute decompensation when PGE1 is discontinued.
explanation.whyCorrect: Critical coarctation of the aorta in neonates is a ductal-dependent lesion where lower body perfusion relies on right-to-left flow through the PDA. When the ductus closes, lower body perfusion is critically compromised, causing absent femoral pulses, metabolic acidosis, and shock. Differential cyanosis (pre-ductal SpO2 > post-ductal SpO2) confirms that deoxygenated blood from the PA is supplying the descending aorta through the ductus.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Interrupted aortic arch presents similarly but involves a complete discontinuity of the aortic arch. While both are ductal-dependent, interrupted arch typically presents earlier, often has associated DiGeorge syndrome (22q11), and usually requires more complex surgical reconstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A large VSD produces a left-to-right shunt with a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border and symptoms of heart failure (tachypnea, poor feeding), but does not cause absent femoral pulses or an upper-to-lower extremity BP gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome presents with severe cyanosis, a single S2, and shock when the ductus closes. However, there would not be a differential between upper and lower extremity pulses—both would be weak—because the entire systemic circulation is ductal-dependent.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return presents with cyanosis and respiratory distress but would not cause absent femoral pulses or an upper-to-lower extremity blood pressure gradient.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child with a small, restrictive muscular ventricular septal defect has been followed with serial echocardiograms. At the initial diagnosis at age 2 months, the VSD measured 3 mm. Current echocardiography shows the defect has decreased to 1.5 mm with trivial left-to-right shunting, a Qp:Qs of 1.1:1, and normal chamber sizes. The child is asymptomatic, growing well, and meeting all developmental milestones. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Refer for surgical VSD closure to prevent endocarditis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate diuretic therapy to prevent volume overload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continued observation as the defect is likely to close spontaneously

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform cardiac catheterization to assess pulmonary vascular resistance

choices.label: E
choices.text: Begin antibiotic prophylaxis prior to dental procedures

explanation.summary: Small muscular VSDs have a high rate of spontaneous closure (up to 80% by age 10). This child's defect is decreasing in size with minimal hemodynamic significance, and observation is the appropriate management.
explanation.whyCorrect: Small, restrictive muscular VSDs commonly close spontaneously during childhood through progressive muscular hypertrophy and apposition of septal tissue. With a Qp:Qs of only 1.1:1 and a shrinking defect, there is no hemodynamic significance requiring intervention. Serial observation with periodic echocardiography is appropriate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surgical closure is not indicated for small, hemodynamically insignificant VSDs that are decreasing in size. The operative risk would outweigh any benefit. Surgery is reserved for large defects with significant shunts or heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Diuretic therapy is unnecessary when there is no evidence of volume overload (normal chamber sizes, Qp:Qs 1.1:1). The child is asymptomatic with normal growth.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac catheterization is invasive and unnecessary in this context. It is indicated when noninvasive assessment is inconclusive regarding pulmonary pressures or when Eisenmenger physiology is suspected—neither applies here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Current AHA guidelines (2007, reaffirmed 2021) do not recommend endocarditis prophylaxis for isolated VSDs unless there is a prior history of endocarditis or the VSD is adjacent to prosthetic material.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with Down syndrome undergoes echocardiography that reveals a complete atrioventricular septal defect with a large primum ASD, a large inlet VSD, and a common AV valve. The Qp:Qs is 3:1 and estimated pulmonary artery pressure is 60 mmHg. The infant has failure to thrive and recurrent lower respiratory tract infections. The surgical team recommends repair. Which of the following postoperative complications is this patient most at risk for after surgical correction of a complete AVSD?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery aneurysm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Supravalvular aortic stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tricuspid valve stenosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary valve endocarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left AV valve (mitral) regurgitation

explanation.summary: The most common postoperative complication after complete AVSD repair is residual or recurrent left AV valve (mitral) regurgitation. The cleft in the left AV valve is a hallmark of AVSD, and despite surgical repair of the cleft, regurgitation may persist or recur.
explanation.whyCorrect: During AVSD repair, the common AV valve is divided into separate left and right AV valves, and the cleft in the anterior leaflet of the left AV valve is closed. However, the abnormal valve morphology (three leaflets rather than the normal two, abnormal papillary muscle arrangement) predisposes to residual regurgitation. Up to 10–15% of patients require reoperation for significant left AV valve regurgitation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary artery aneurysms are associated with Kawasaki disease, not AVSD repair. There is no mechanism by which AVSD surgery would cause coronary artery pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Supravalvular aortic stenosis is associated with Williams syndrome, not AVSD repair. It is caused by elastin gene deletion and is not a surgical complication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tricuspid (right AV) valve stenosis is not a common complication of AVSD repair. The right-sided valve is generally less problematic than the left side after repair.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary valve endocarditis is not a recognized complication of AVSD repair. The pulmonary valve is not part of the surgical repair.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with Eisenmenger syndrome secondary to an unrepaired large VSD presents to the obstetrics clinic at 8 weeks gestation. She reports worsening dyspnea and her baseline oxygen saturation is 80% on room air. Hemoglobin is 19.5 g/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate counseling regarding this pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pregnancy is safe with close monitoring in the third trimester

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgical VSD closure should be performed during the second trimester

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pregnancy carries a maternal mortality risk exceeding 30% and termination should be discussed

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bed rest and supplemental oxygen are sufficient to mitigate pregnancy risks

choices.label: E
choices.text: Initiation of warfarin anticoagulation will reduce the risk of paradoxical embolism

explanation.summary: Eisenmenger syndrome carries the highest maternal mortality risk of any cardiac condition during pregnancy (30–50%). The physiologic changes of pregnancy (decreased SVR, increased blood volume, hypercoagulability) worsen the right-to-left shunt and can precipitate fatal cardiovascular collapse.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pregnancy in Eisenmenger syndrome is classified as WHO class IV (extremely high risk, pregnancy contraindicated). The decrease in systemic vascular resistance during pregnancy increases right-to-left shunting and worsens cyanosis. The peripartum period is particularly dangerous due to fluid shifts and hemorrhage risk. Maternal mortality ranges from 30–50%, and termination of pregnancy should be discussed early.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pregnancy is absolutely not safe in Eisenmenger syndrome. Even with intensive monitoring, the physiologic stresses of pregnancy cause unpredictable, life-threatening hemodynamic deterioration.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Surgical VSD closure is contraindicated once Eisenmenger physiology has developed. The irreversible pulmonary vascular disease means that closing the defect would eliminate the pressure 'pop-off' for the right ventricle, causing acute right heart failure and death.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bed rest and supplemental oxygen do not adequately mitigate the extreme hemodynamic risks. The fundamental problem—fixed elevated pulmonary vascular resistance with right-to-left shunting—cannot be managed with conservative measures alone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin is teratogenic (category X), especially in the first trimester, causing warfarin embryopathy (nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses). While anticoagulation may be considered with heparin products for thromboembolic risk, it does not address the fundamental hemodynamic danger.

questions.stem: A newborn male is noted to have progressive cyanosis within the first few hours of life. Oxygen saturation is 65% on 100% FiO2 by face mask and does not improve with supplemental oxygen. The infant appears tachypneic but has no respiratory distress. Chest radiograph shows an egg-shaped cardiac silhouette with a narrow mediastinum and increased pulmonary vascular markings. An urgent echocardiogram is obtained. While awaiting definitive intervention, which of the following is the most critical initial pharmacologic therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous indomethacin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhaled nitric oxide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous prostaglandin E1

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous epinephrine infusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sodium bicarbonate bolus

explanation.summary: The egg-shaped heart (egg-on-a-string) with narrow mediastinum is classic for transposition of the great arteries (d-TGA). This is a ductal-dependent cyanotic lesion requiring PGE1 to maintain ductal patency and allow mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood while awaiting balloon atrial septostomy and subsequent arterial switch operation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In d-TGA, the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery from the left ventricle, creating parallel (rather than series) circuits. Survival depends on mixing through the PDA, foramen ovale, or both. PGE1 maintains ductal patency, improving intercirculatory mixing and increasing oxygen saturation. This is the critical bridge to balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind procedure) and later the arterial switch operation (Jatene procedure).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Indomethacin promotes ductal closure and would be catastrophic in this ductal-dependent lesion. Closing the PDA would eliminate the only mixing point and lead to refractory hypoxemia and death.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Inhaled nitric oxide reduces pulmonary vascular resistance and is used for persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn. In TGA, the problem is parallel circulations with inadequate mixing, not elevated PVR. Nitric oxide would not address the fundamental issue.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epinephrine is used for hemodynamic support in cardiogenic shock but does not address the mixing physiology needed in TGA. It would increase myocardial oxygen demand without improving oxygen delivery.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sodium bicarbonate may transiently correct metabolic acidosis but does not address the underlying cause of cyanosis. It is a temporizing measure and not a definitive intervention for TGA.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant with known tetralogy of Fallot is brought to the emergency department because of an episode of inconsolable crying followed by deep cyanosis and limpness. The mother states the infant turned 'blue all over' for approximately 10 minutes. On arrival, the infant is irritable, with oxygen saturation of 70% on room air. The previously heard systolic murmur is now inaudible. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent surgical complete repair of tetralogy of Fallot

choices.label: B
choices.text: Knee-to-chest positioning, oxygen, and intravenous morphine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate intubation and inhaled nitric oxide

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous furosemide and fluid restriction

explanation.summary: This infant is having a hypercyanotic ('tet') spell—acute dynamic infundibular spasm causing increased right-to-left shunting. The murmur disappears because there is no flow across the obstructed RVOT. Knee-to-chest positioning increases SVR, while morphine reduces agitation and relaxes the infundibulum.
explanation.whyCorrect: The initial management of a tet spell includes: (1) knee-to-chest positioning to increase systemic vascular resistance and reduce right-to-left shunting; (2) supplemental oxygen; (3) IV morphine to reduce agitation and relax the infundibular muscle; (4) IV fluid bolus to increase preload. If these measures fail, IV phenylephrine (alpha-1 agonist) can be used to increase SVR. The murmur disappears during the spell because nearly all RV output is shunted across the VSD rather than through the obstructed RVOT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Emergent surgery is not the immediate management of a tet spell. While the episode may accelerate plans for definitive surgical repair, the acute spell must first be managed medically. Emergent BT shunt may be considered if medical management fails.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhaled nitric oxide reduces pulmonary vascular resistance and is not indicated in TOF. The problem is dynamic infundibular obstruction, not elevated PVR. Reducing PVR in the presence of a VSD could worsen the right-to-left shunt.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PGE1 is used in neonates with ductal-dependent lesions. At 4 months, the ductus is typically closed and PGE1 would be ineffective. The acute problem in a tet spell is dynamic infundibular spasm, not ductal closure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Furosemide and fluid restriction would decrease preload, reducing right ventricular filling and worsening the dynamic obstruction. Volume expansion, not depletion, is part of tet spell management.

questions.stem: A 3-day-old newborn presents with severe cyanosis that does not improve with supplemental oxygen. Echocardiography reveals d-transposition of the great arteries with an intact ventricular septum and a restrictive patent foramen ovale. The infant's oxygen saturation remains 55% despite PGE1 infusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent arterial switch operation (Jatene procedure)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrial switch operation (Mustard procedure)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Placement of a Blalock-Taussig shunt

choices.label: D
choices.text: Balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind procedure)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation

explanation.summary: In d-TGA with intact ventricular septum and a restrictive PFO, the PDA alone may not provide adequate mixing. Balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind procedure) creates a larger atrial communication to improve intercirculatory mixing, serving as a critical bridge to the arterial switch operation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy is performed at the bedside using echocardiographic guidance. A balloon catheter is advanced through the femoral vein into the left atrium via the PFO, inflated, and rapidly pulled back to tear the atrial septum. This creates a nonrestrictive atrial communication, dramatically improving oxygen saturation by enhancing mixing between the parallel circuits. The definitive arterial switch operation is then performed electively within the first 2 weeks of life.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The arterial switch operation is the definitive repair but is performed semi-electively (within the first 2 weeks). The infant's acute, life-threatening hypoxemia must be addressed first with balloon septostomy to stabilize the patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The Mustard/Senning atrial switch procedures are historical operations that redirect venous return at the atrial level. They have been largely replaced by the arterial switch operation, which provides anatomic correction and better long-term outcomes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A Blalock-Taussig shunt (systemic-to-pulmonary artery shunt) is used to augment pulmonary blood flow in lesions with decreased pulmonary flow (e.g., TOF, pulmonary atresia). In TGA, pulmonary blood flow is already adequate; the problem is inadequate mixing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ECMO is reserved as a rescue therapy for refractory cardiopulmonary failure and is not the appropriate next step when a simpler, definitive intervention (balloon septostomy) can immediately improve mixing.

questions.stem: A 2-month-old infant is evaluated for cyanosis and failure to thrive. Echocardiography reveals a single ventricle with a common atrioventricular valve, transposition of the great arteries, and pulmonary stenosis. After initial stabilization with a Blalock-Taussig shunt in the neonatal period, the pediatric cardiologist discusses the staged surgical approach. Which of the following is the correct sequence of palliative surgical procedures for single ventricle physiology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Norwood → Fontan → Glenn

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glenn → Norwood → Fontan

choices.label: C
choices.text: Norwood → Glenn → Fontan

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fontan → Glenn → Norwood

choices.label: E
choices.text: Glenn → Fontan → Norwood

explanation.summary: The staged palliation for single ventricle physiology follows the sequence: Stage 1 (Norwood, neonatal period) → Stage 2 (bidirectional Glenn/hemi-Fontan, 4–6 months) → Stage 3 (Fontan completion, 2–4 years).
explanation.whyCorrect: The Norwood procedure (Stage 1) is performed in the neonatal period to establish reliable systemic blood flow and control pulmonary blood flow. The bidirectional Glenn (Stage 2) at 4–6 months connects the SVC directly to the pulmonary artery, reducing volume load on the single ventricle. The Fontan procedure (Stage 3) at 2–4 years connects the IVC to the pulmonary artery, completing the passive pulmonary circulation so that all systemic venous return flows directly to the lungs without a ventricular pump.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This sequence incorrectly places the Fontan before the Glenn. The Glenn is an intermediate step that allows the pulmonary vasculature to adapt to passive flow before the Fontan completion. Performing the Fontan directly after Norwood would impose too great a volume load change.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The Glenn cannot be performed first because the neonate requires the Norwood to establish adequate systemic circulation. The Glenn requires stable systemic output and low pulmonary vascular resistance, which takes months to develop.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This sequence is completely reversed. The Fontan is the final stage, not the first. The Norwood is the initial procedure performed in the neonatal period.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This sequence omits the critical first step (Norwood) needed to establish systemic blood flow. Without initial surgical reconstruction, the infant would not survive to undergo the Glenn procedure.

questions.stem: A 10-day-old neonate presents with severe cyanosis, tachypnea, and poor feeding. Oxygen saturation is 72% and does not improve with 100% FiO2. Chest radiograph shows a 'snowman' or 'figure-of-eight' cardiac silhouette with increased pulmonary vascular markings. Echocardiography reveals that all four pulmonary veins drain into a vertical vein that connects to the left innominate vein and then to the superior vena cava. An obligatory right-to-left shunt is present at the atrial level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Truncus arteriosus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (supracardiac type)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tricuspid atresia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ebstein anomaly

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome

explanation.summary: The 'snowman' (figure-of-eight) silhouette is pathognomonic for supracardiac total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR), where all pulmonary veins drain into a vertical vein → left innominate vein → SVC → right atrium, creating the widened superior mediastinum that forms the 'snowman's head.'
explanation.whyCorrect: In supracardiac TAPVR, all pulmonary venous blood returns to the right atrium via the SVC. The dilated vertical vein and SVC create the widened superior mediastinum (snowman's head), while the heart forms the body. Since no pulmonary venous blood reaches the left atrium directly, an ASD or PFO is obligatory for left heart filling and systemic output. Surgical correction involves anastomosis of the pulmonary venous confluence to the left atrium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Truncus arteriosus presents with a single great vessel overriding both ventricles, cyanosis, and a systolic ejection murmur. The chest radiograph typically shows cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary markings but not a snowman silhouette.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tricuspid atresia presents with cyanosis, a hypoplastic right ventricle, and obligatory left-to-right shunt at the atrial level (right-to-left through an ASD). Chest radiograph shows decreased pulmonary vascular markings in most cases, not the snowman sign.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ebstein anomaly involves apical displacement of the tricuspid valve with atrialization of the right ventricle. CXR shows massive cardiomegaly ('wall-to-wall heart'), not a snowman silhouette.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome presents with cyanosis and shock when the ductus closes but has a different CXR appearance with cardiomegaly and pulmonary edema, not the snowman sign.

questions.stem: A 6-hour-old newborn is found to have central cyanosis and oxygen saturation of 75%. The infant was born to a mother with poorly controlled gestational diabetes. Echocardiography reveals that the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery arises from the left ventricle. The ventricular septum is intact. Which of the following embryologic mechanisms is most likely responsible for this defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral during truncal division

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure of endocardial cushion fusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abnormal neural crest cell migration to the outflow tract

choices.label: D
choices.text: Premature closure of the foramen ovale

choices.label: E
choices.text: Failure of the bulbus cordis to resorb

explanation.summary: D-transposition of the great arteries results from failure of the aorticopulmonary (conotruncal) septum to undergo its normal spiral (approximately 180°) during cardiac development, resulting in the aorta and pulmonary artery arising from the wrong ventricles.
explanation.whyCorrect: During normal development, the conotruncal septum spirals as it divides the truncus arteriosus into the aorta and pulmonary artery, ensuring that the aorta connects to the left ventricle and the PA to the right ventricle. In d-TGA, the septum forms straight (without spiraling), so the aorta remains connected to the anterior right ventricle and the PA to the posterior left ventricle, creating parallel rather than series circulations.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Failure of endocardial cushion fusion results in atrioventricular septal defects (primum ASD, inlet VSD, common AV valve), which are associated with Down syndrome. This mechanism does not affect great vessel positioning.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neural crest cell migration abnormalities cause conotruncal defects such as persistent truncus arteriosus, tetralogy of Fallot, and interrupted aortic arch (associated with DiGeorge syndrome). While neural crest cells contribute to outflow tract development, TGA is specifically due to failure of septal spiraling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Premature closure of the foramen ovale can cause hypoplastic left heart syndrome by restricting left atrial flow and left ventricular development, not transposition of the great vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The bulbus cordis contributes to the right ventricular outflow tract. Its failure to resorb is not the mechanism underlying TGA; rather, it is the orientation of the conotruncal septum that determines great vessel connections.

questions.stem: A 3-month-old infant presents with gradually worsening cyanosis. Echocardiography reveals a large VSD with overriding aorta, right ventricular hypertrophy, and severe infundibular and valvular pulmonary stenosis with a pulmonary annulus Z-score of -4. The infant weighs 3.8 kg and has oxygen saturations of 72%. The surgical team determines that primary complete repair carries excessive risk due to the severely hypoplastic pulmonary annulus. Which of the following palliative procedures is most appropriate as an initial surgical intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulmonary artery band placement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Modified Blalock-Taussig shunt

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bidirectional Glenn anastomosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rastelli procedure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Damus-Kaye-Stansel procedure

explanation.summary: A modified Blalock-Taussig (mBT) shunt is the appropriate palliative procedure for TOF with severe pulmonary stenosis/hypoplastic pulmonary annulus when complete repair is not feasible. It provides a reliable source of pulmonary blood flow by connecting the subclavian artery to the pulmonary artery via a Gore-Tex graft.
explanation.whyCorrect: The modified BT shunt uses a Gore-Tex interposition graft between the subclavian (or innominate) artery and the ipsilateral pulmonary artery. This provides controlled pulmonary blood flow, improves oxygenation, and promotes pulmonary artery growth, allowing definitive complete repair at a later date when the child is larger and the pulmonary arteries have grown.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulmonary artery banding restricts pulmonary blood flow and is used in lesions with excessive pulmonary flow (e.g., large VSD without pulmonary stenosis). In TOF, pulmonary flow is already restricted by the RVOT obstruction; banding would be catastrophic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The bidirectional Glenn connects the SVC to the PA and is used in single ventricle palliation (Stage 2). It is not appropriate for TOF, which has two functional ventricles and will undergo biventricular repair.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The Rastelli procedure involves placing an RV-to-PA conduit and closing the VSD. It is used for TOF with pulmonary atresia or in certain TGA variants but is a more definitive procedure typically performed when the child is larger, not as an initial palliative procedure in a 3.8-kg infant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The Damus-Kaye-Stansel procedure connects the proximal main pulmonary artery to the ascending aorta, used in complex single ventricle lesions with subaortic obstruction. It is not applicable to standard TOF physiology.

questions.stem: A 2-day-old neonate presents with severe cyanosis, respiratory distress, and metabolic acidosis. Prostaglandin E1 is initiated. Echocardiography reveals a severely hypoplastic left ventricle with mitral atresia, aortic atresia, and a hypoplastic ascending aorta. Systemic blood flow is entirely dependent on right-to-left flow through a large PDA. Which of the following best describes the initial surgical management for this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Biventricular repair with aortic and mitral valve replacement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orthotopic heart transplantation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Norwood procedure with systemic-to-pulmonary artery shunt

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arterial switch operation with VSD closure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bidirectional Glenn with atrial septectomy

explanation.summary: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS) requires staged single-ventricle palliation. The Norwood procedure (Stage 1) is performed in the neonatal period to reconstruct the aortic arch using the native pulmonary artery, create an unobstructed systemic outflow, and provide controlled pulmonary blood flow.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Norwood procedure involves: (1) dividing the main PA and connecting the proximal PA to the neo-aorta (reconstructed with the hypoplastic native aorta and homograft); (2) atrial septectomy to ensure unrestricted pulmonary venous return to the right atrium; (3) establishing pulmonary blood flow via a modified BT shunt or Sano shunt (RV-to-PA conduit). This creates a single right ventricle-dependent circulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Biventricular repair is impossible because the left ventricle, mitral valve, and aortic valve are severely hypoplastic or atretic. There is insufficient left-sided heart structure to support a biventricular circulation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Heart transplantation is an alternative to the Norwood pathway but is not the standard initial approach due to donor organ scarcity for neonates and long wait times. It may be considered for high-risk patients or those who fail staged palliation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The arterial switch operation is for d-TGA, not HLHS. It requires two functional ventricles and does not address the fundamental problem of HLHS—absence of a functional left ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The bidirectional Glenn is Stage 2 of single-ventricle palliation, performed at 4–6 months of age. It cannot be performed first because the neonate's pulmonary vascular resistance is too high for passive pulmonary blood flow, and the systemic circulation has not yet been reconstructed.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy with repaired tetralogy of Fallot (complete repair at age 6 months) presents for routine follow-up. He is asymptomatic but has noticed decreasing exercise tolerance over the past year. On examination, there is a grade 3/6 low-pitched diastolic decrescendo murmur at the left upper sternal border. ECG shows a QRS duration of 170 ms with a right bundle branch block pattern. Cardiac MRI reveals severe right ventricular dilation with a right ventricular end-diastolic volume index of 170 mL/m² and a right ventricular ejection fraction of 38%. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Residual ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acquired pulmonary artery branch stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recurrent infundibular stenosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic root dilation with aortic regurgitation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Severe pulmonary regurgitation from the prior RVOT patch repair

explanation.summary: Chronic severe pulmonary regurgitation is the most common long-term sequela after TOF repair with transannular patch. The diastolic murmur at the left upper sternal border, progressive RV dilation, RV dysfunction, and prolonged QRS duration are classic findings indicating the need for pulmonary valve replacement.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transannular patch repair of TOF relieves RVOT obstruction but sacrifices pulmonary valve competence, leading to 'free' pulmonary regurgitation. Over years, the chronic volume overload causes progressive RV dilation and dysfunction. A QRS duration >180 ms is a risk factor for ventricular arrhythmias and sudden death. Pulmonary valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter) is indicated for severe PR with RV dilation (RVEDVI >160 mL/m²) or declining RV function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A residual VSD with left-to-right shunting would cause a holosystolic murmur, not a diastolic decrescendo murmur. It would cause left ventricular volume overload and left atrial dilation, not isolated RV dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Branch pulmonary artery stenosis produces a continuous murmur radiating to the back and lung fields. While it can cause RV pressure overload, the diastolic murmur at the left upper sternal border and RV volume overload pattern are more consistent with pulmonary regurgitation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Recurrent infundibular stenosis would produce a systolic ejection murmur, not a diastolic murmur. It would cause RV hypertrophy and pressure overload rather than the volume-overloaded, dilated RV seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic root dilation does occur after TOF repair but presents with an early diastolic murmur at the right sternal border. It causes LV dilation, not RV dilation. The cardiac MRI findings point specifically to RV pathology.

questions.stem: A 1-day-old neonate with known prenatal diagnosis of tricuspid atresia is evaluated. Echocardiography confirms tricuspid atresia with normally related great arteries, a restrictive VSD, and reduced pulmonary blood flow. The oxygen saturation is 70% on room air. Prostaglandin E1 is started. Which of the following ECG findings is most expected in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Right axis deviation with right ventricular hypertrophy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left axis deviation with left ventricular hypertrophy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal axis with biventricular hypertrophy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right axis deviation with right atrial enlargement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Extreme axis deviation (northwest axis) with low voltage

explanation.summary: Tricuspid atresia classically shows left axis deviation (superior axis) and left ventricular hypertrophy on ECG. Because the tricuspid valve is atretic, the right ventricle is hypoplastic, and all systemic and pulmonary venous blood must pass through the left atrium and left ventricle.
explanation.whyCorrect: In tricuspid atresia, the absent tricuspid valve prevents blood from entering the right ventricle, which becomes hypoplastic. All blood flows from the right atrium through an obligatory ASD to the left atrium and left ventricle. The LV handles the entire cardiac output, leading to LVH. The superior (leftward) axis results from the hypoplastic RV and abnormal conduction through the hypoplastic ventricular septum. This ECG pattern (left axis + LVH in a cyanotic neonate) is nearly pathognomonic for tricuspid atresia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Right axis deviation with RVH is the opposite of what is expected. It would be seen in conditions with RV pressure or volume overload (e.g., ASD, tetralogy of Fallot, pulmonary hypertension). The hypoplastic RV in tricuspid atresia cannot generate right-sided forces.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Biventricular hypertrophy would suggest both ventricles are significantly loaded, as in a large VSD or AVSD. In tricuspid atresia, the RV is hypoplastic and cannot develop hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Right atrial enlargement (tall P waves in lead II) may be present but right axis deviation would not be expected. The dominant ECG feature is left axis deviation due to the hypoplastic RV and LVH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Northwest axis (extreme axis deviation) is associated with AV septal defects or ventricular tachycardia. Low voltage suggests pericardial effusion or myocarditis. Neither pattern is characteristic of tricuspid atresia.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy with a history of uncorrected tetralogy of Fallot is brought to the emergency department after his parents noticed he was squatting during play. The child frequently assumes a squatting position when playing with other children. Which of the following best explains the physiologic benefit of squatting in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreases venous return to the right heart, reducing right-to-left shunting

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enhances left ventricular contractility through the Frank-Starling mechanism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases pulmonary vascular resistance, directing more blood through the lungs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increases systemic vascular resistance, reducing right-to-left shunting across the VSD

choices.label: E
choices.text: Compresses the inferior vena cava, reducing preload and oxygen consumption

explanation.summary: Squatting kinks the large femoral arteries, acutely increasing systemic vascular resistance (SVR). This increases left ventricular and aortic pressure relative to right ventricular pressure, thereby reducing the right-to-left shunt across the VSD and directing more deoxygenated blood through the RVOT into the lungs.
explanation.whyCorrect: In TOF, the degree of right-to-left shunting is determined by the ratio of pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) plus RVOT obstruction to systemic vascular resistance (SVR). By increasing SVR through femoral artery compression, squatting makes it relatively easier for blood to flow through the obstructed RVOT into the pulmonary circulation rather than shunting right-to-left. This increases pulmonary blood flow and improves oxygenation. Squatting also increases venous return by compressing venous capacitance in the legs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Squatting actually increases venous return by compressing the venous capacitance beds in the legs. Decreased venous return would reduce cardiac output and worsen hemodynamics.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While squatting does increase preload somewhat (which may mildly improve LV output via Frank-Starling), the primary and most important mechanism is the increase in SVR that reduces the right-to-left shunt fraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased PVR would worsen right-to-left shunting, not improve it. The beneficial effect of squatting is through increasing SVR, not PVR.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Squatting compresses the femoral arteries (increasing SVR), not primarily the IVC. It actually increases rather than decreases venous return. Reducing preload would worsen cardiac output.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy with a history of Fontan palliation completed at age 3 for tricuspid atresia presents with progressive abdominal distension, peripheral edema, and protein-losing enteropathy. Serum albumin is 1.8 g/dL. His Fontan circuit pressures are elevated at 18 mmHg. Which of the following pathophysiologic mechanisms best explains this patient's protein-losing enteropathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hepatic synthetic failure from cardiac cirrhosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic mesenteric venous hypertension causing intestinal lymphatic dysfunction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Autoimmune enteropathy triggered by chronic cyanosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Malabsorption from celiac disease associated with congenital heart defects

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nephrotic syndrome from chronic renal hypoperfusion

explanation.summary: Protein-losing enteropathy (PLE) is a devastating complication affecting 5–15% of Fontan patients. Elevated Fontan circuit pressures cause chronic mesenteric venous hypertension, leading to intestinal lymphatic congestion, inflammation, and loss of protein-rich lymphatic fluid into the intestinal lumen.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Fontan physiology, systemic venous pressure is chronically elevated because the passive pulmonary blood flow circuit lacks a subpulmonary ventricle. Elevated central venous pressure is transmitted to the mesenteric veins and intestinal lymphatics, causing lymphatic dilation, dysfunction, and leakage of protein-rich lymph into the gut lumen. The resulting hypoalbuminemia causes peripheral edema, ascites, and immunodeficiency (from immunoglobulin loss). Treatment is challenging and may ultimately require heart transplantation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While Fontan patients do develop cardiac cirrhosis over time from chronic hepatic venous congestion, the primary mechanism of PLE is intestinal lymphatic dysfunction, not hepatic synthetic failure. The liver can maintain albumin synthesis unless cirrhosis is advanced.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no established autoimmune mechanism for PLE in Fontan patients. The pathophysiology is primarily hemodynamic (elevated venous pressures) and lymphatic, not immunologic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Celiac disease is not associated with congenital heart defects or Fontan palliation. PLE in Fontan patients is a distinct entity caused by hemodynamic and lymphatic mechanisms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While renal dysfunction can occur in Fontan patients from chronic low cardiac output, nephrotic syndrome presents with proteinuria and lipiduria. The protein loss in Fontan-associated PLE is gastrointestinal, not renal.

questions.stem: A 5-day-old neonate born to a mother with poorly controlled pregestational diabetes presents with cyanosis and respiratory distress. Echocardiography shows a single arterial trunk overriding both ventricles, a large outlet VSD directly beneath the trunk, and a single semilunar (truncal) valve with four leaflets. No separate pulmonary artery origin from the heart is identified; instead, the branch pulmonary arteries arise directly from the common trunk. Which of the following embryologic defects is responsible for this malformation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure of aorticopulmonary septation due to abnormal neural crest cell migration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure of endocardial cushion development

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abnormal looping of the primitive heart tube

choices.label: D
choices.text: Failure of septum primum to fuse with the endocardial cushions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Premature regression of the left fourth aortic arch

explanation.summary: Persistent truncus arteriosus results from complete failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to develop, leaving a single great vessel that supplies the systemic, pulmonary, and coronary circulations. This is caused by abnormal neural crest cell migration to the conotruncal region.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neural crest cells are critical for aorticopulmonary septation. When their migration is disrupted (as in DiGeorge syndrome/22q11 deletion), the conotruncal septum fails to form, resulting in a persistent single trunk. The single truncal valve (often dysplastic with 2–5 leaflets) sits above a large outlet VSD. Truncus arteriosus is classified by the origin of the pulmonary arteries from the common trunk (Type I–IV, Collett-Edwards classification).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Endocardial cushion defects cause atrioventricular septal defects (primum ASD, inlet VSD, common AV valve), not truncus arteriosus. The endocardial cushions are involved in AV valve and septal formation, not outflow tract septation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Abnormal looping of the heart tube results in dextrocardia or situs inversus, not truncus arteriosus. Looping determines the left-right orientation of cardiac chambers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Failure of the septum primum to fuse with endocardial cushions results in a primum ASD (ostium primum defect), not truncus arteriosus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Premature regression of the left fourth aortic arch would result in a right-sided aortic arch or interrupted aortic arch, not persistent truncus arteriosus. The fourth arch gives rise to the definitive aortic arch.

questions.stem: A 2-week-old infant presents with progressively worsening cyanosis and feeding difficulty. Echocardiography reveals severe downward displacement of the septal and posterior tricuspid valve leaflets into the right ventricle, with a massively dilated right atrium. The functional right ventricular cavity is severely reduced. An ASD with right-to-left shunting is present. Chest radiograph shows massive cardiomegaly with a 'wall-to-wall' heart appearance and decreased pulmonary vascular markings. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tricuspid atresia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot with absent pulmonary valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ebstein anomaly

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulmonary atresia with intact ventricular septum

explanation.summary: Ebstein anomaly is characterized by apical displacement of the septal and posterior tricuspid valve leaflets, 'atrialization' of a portion of the right ventricle, massive right atrial dilation, and a wall-to-wall heart on CXR. The functional RV is small, and right-to-left shunting occurs through an ASD or PFO.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Ebstein anomaly, the septal and posterior leaflets of the tricuspid valve are displaced apically into the RV, creating an 'atrialized' portion of the RV that functions as part of the right atrium. This results in massive RA dilation, severe tricuspid regurgitation, a small functional RV, and reduced forward pulmonary blood flow. The enormous right atrium produces the characteristic 'wall-to-wall' cardiac silhouette on CXR. Cyanosis results from right-to-left shunting through the ASD. It is associated with maternal lithium exposure during pregnancy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tricuspid atresia involves absence of the tricuspid valve with a hypoplastic RV. The tricuspid valve is present (though displaced) in Ebstein anomaly. Tricuspid atresia shows left axis deviation and LVH on ECG, whereas Ebstein shows right atrial enlargement and RBBB.
whyWrongByChoice.B: TOF with absent pulmonary valve presents with massive pulmonary artery dilation causing airway compression (respiratory distress) and a to-and-fro murmur. The characteristic findings are dilated PAs, not a wall-to-wall heart from RA dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TAPVR presents with cyanosis and may have a 'snowman' sign on CXR (supracardiac type) or normal heart size with pulmonary edema (infracardiac type). It does not feature massive cardiomegaly from right atrial dilation or displaced tricuspid valve leaflets.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary atresia with intact ventricular septum presents with cyanosis and ductal-dependent pulmonary blood flow, but the tricuspid valve is in the normal position (though often regurgitant). The RV may be hypertrophied rather than atrialized, and CXR shows decreased pulmonary markings without the characteristic wall-to-wall heart.
